text: After achieving pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon begins dissection using Maryland monopolar scissors, carefully manipulating the tissue within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon dissects and mobilizes the splenic flexure of the colon, taking down the colosplenic ligaments to facilitate further surgical access.
The surgeon has mobilized the spleen and the tail of the pancreas, removing investing tissue to reveal clear exposure of the adrenal mass.
The surgeon carefully removes the adrenal mass from the tail of the pancreas and the spleen, ensuring clear separation from these structures.
The surgeon initiates a vein-first approach for the removal of a pheochromocytoma, focusing on the dissection of veins in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the renal vein and observes the adrenal vein entering the renal vein, highlighting the anatomical connection between these structures.
The surgeon dissects the adrenal vein away from surrounding tissues, using hemoclips to isolate and subsequently divide the vein.
The surgeon isolates a second adrenal vein, applies hemoclips, and divides the vein, with clear visualization of the procedure.
The surgeon meticulously dissects the mass away from the underlying structures, ensuring careful separation to avoid damage to adjacent tissues.
The spleen is visible on the left side of the screen, while the kidney is located at the bottom of the dissection plane.

The surgeon places the dissected mass into an endocatch bag, which is then tagged to the abdominal wall for later removal after patient repositioning and completion of the procedure on the opposite side.
The right flank, abdomen, and back are prepped and draped in a sterile manner. The procedure begins with the takedown of the hepatic flexure and mobilization of the duodenum.

The adrenal mass is visible, and a liver retractor is used to retract the liver cephalically for better exposure. The surgeon begins careful dissection of the mass from the investing and underlying tissues using electrocautery.

The surgeon encounters difficulty performing the vein-first approach on the right side due to the adrenal tumor's position directly atop the inferior vena cava (IVC), obstructing clear access to the vein.

The surgeon incises Girota's fascia to locate the superior pole of the kidney. The adrenal gland is separated from the superior pole, followed by a posterior dissection revealing the diaphragm and psoas muscle.
The surgeon identifies small arterial branches in the surgical field, likely near the adrenal mass, using careful dissection techniques.
The surgeon clips small arterial branches using a hemoclip device, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon cauterizes the side of the adrenal tumor using monopolar scissors before cutting through the tissue, visibly separating the tumor from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein, visually distinguishing it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon visually examines the adrenal vein, noting its short and thick appearance.
Two hemoclips are placed on the side of the adrenal vein that will remain in situ, followed by the placement of an additional hemoclip on the side of the specimen to be resected.
The surgeon divides the adrenal vein, ensuring adequate hemostasis is achieved, with no visible bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the final attachments of the adrenal gland, freeing it from surrounding tissues. The gland is then placed into an endo-catch bag for removal from the abdominal cavity.
